title: Meshlet V2 - Compact & Versatile RP2350 Node

messages.author: dj505
messages.text: V1 isn't worth posting, lol. Meshlet is a very small RP2350-based LoRa mesh network node that includes everything I want out of a very small node. This includes: USB C, with configurable charge rate limit (250mA, 500mA, 1A) LiPo/LiIon and LiFePo4 support ESD/TVS protection on the USB port Solar input (as well as constant DC input) from 3.3V to 18V, tolerant up to 25V Flexible wiring methods, with dedicated JST PH footprints as well as pads to directly solder wires to! Tested & working with as little as a cheap 80mm 5v solar panel, on an overcast Canadian winter day! Unlike many boards that need a manual reset after too little sunlight drains the battery, the Meshlet should recover on its own! Smart power path management, thanks to the BQ25185. No need to worry about unnecessarily cycling the battery! JST 2.0 and Picoblade 1.5mm battery connector footprints, to hopefully fit whatever LiPos you've got laying around Low operating power, measuring as low as 28mA idle current draw (without WiFi) Active current/voltage monitoring and reporting on 3 channels over the mesh, via INA3221 Up to 8MB onboard flash WIO-SX1262 LoRa radio BME280 temperature/humidity/barometric pressure sensor I2C expansion pins Dedicated 3.3V regulators for all major components, each with its own self-resetting fuse, to ensure stable power "External notification" header for a MOSFET-driven 3.3V buzzer, LED, vibration motor, etc Raspberry Pi RM2 for optional WiFi (and Bluetooth, whenever the firmware supports it) Total footprint size of ~42*32mm, less than half the size of a credit card! I just got MeshCore up and running on this thing and will be doing some extensive testing over the next few weeks. Excited to see how it holds up as a repeater!
messages.content_html:
V1 isn't worth posting, lol. Meshlet is a very small RP2350-based LoRa mesh network node that includes everything I want out of a very small node. This includes:
  • USB C, with configurable charge rate limit (250mA, 500mA, 1A)
  • LiPo/LiIon and LiFePo4 support
  • ESD/TVS protection on the USB port
  • Solar input (as well as constant DC input) from 3.3V to 18V, tolerant up to 25V
    • Flexible wiring methods, with dedicated JST PH footprints as well as pads to directly solder wires to!
    • Tested & working with as little as a cheap 80mm 5v solar panel, on an overcast Canadian winter day!
    • Unlike many boards that need a manual reset after too little sunlight drains the battery, the Meshlet should recover on its own!
  • Smart power path management, thanks to the BQ25185. No need to worry about unnecessarily cycling the battery!
  • JST 2.0 and Picoblade 1.5mm battery connector footprints, to hopefully fit whatever LiPos you've got laying around
  • Low operating power, measuring as low as 28mA idle current draw (without WiFi)
  • Active current/voltage monitoring and reporting on 3 channels over the mesh, via INA3221
  • Up to 8MB onboard flash
  • WIO-SX1262 LoRa radio
  • BME280 temperature/humidity/barometric pressure sensor
  • I2C expansion pins
  • Dedicated 3.3V regulators for all major components, each with its own self-resetting fuse, to ensure stable power
  • "External notification" header for a MOSFET-driven 3.3V buzzer, LED, vibration motor, etc
  • Raspberry Pi RM2 for optional WiFi (and Bluetooth, whenever the firmware supports it)
  • Total footprint size of ~42*32mm, less than half the size of a credit card! I just got MeshCore up and running on this thing and will be doing some extensive testing over the next few weeks. Excited to see how it holds up as a repeater!
embeds_html:
Meshlet
A Compact & Versatile RP2350 Mesh Network Node

messages.author: beala
messages.text: Nice choice with the BQ25185! I've been looking at the TI charge controllers for a power supply project of my own. Is everything at 3 volts so you run the SYS output to a regulator? or do you have a boost onboard?
messages.content_html: Nice choice with the BQ25185! I've been looking at the TI charge controllers for a power supply project of my own. Is everything at 3 volts so you run the SYS output to a regulator? or do you have a boost onboard?

messages.author: dj505
messages.text: I love how easy the BQ25185 is to use, lol. It outputs roughly 4.5V regulated, which I can pass to a few 3V3 regulators for everything that needs 3V3, and the raw 4.5V output can power the WiFi module (if present)
messages.content_html: I love how easy the BQ25185 is to use, lol. It outputs roughly 4.5V regulated, which I can pass to a few 3V3 regulators for everything that needs 3V3, and the raw 4.5V output can power the WiFi module (if present)

messages.author: dj505
messages.text: I just reread your message when I wasn't as distracted - yea pretty much everything on here runs at 3.3 volts, so the SYS output goes to several regulators, haha
messages.content_html: I just reread your message when I wasn't as distracted - yea pretty much everything on here runs at 3.3 volts, so the SYS output goes to several regulators, haha

beala
Nice choice with the BQ25185! I've been looking at the TI charge controllers for a power supply project of my own. Is everything at 3 volts so you run the SYS output to a regulator? or do you have a boost onboard?

messages.author: dj505
messages.text: Sorta. It's not really something I plan to sell, at least not regularly. The resources to buy them preassembled via PCBWay/etc are all going to be there, and you can totally do it that way, but you could also solder it together yourself cheaper (edited)
messages.content_html: Sorta. It's not really something I plan to sell, at least not regularly. The resources to buy them preassembled via PCBWay/etc are all going to be there, and you can totally do it that way, but you could also solder it together yourself cheaper (edited)
